questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What separates HTTP headers from the body in a request?

choices.text: A blank line (CRLF) between the header block and the body
A semicolon placed after the last header line
A tab character that marks where headers end
The literal delimiter '---' between headers and body

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: Which HTTP header specifies the format of the request body?

choices.text: Accept (content types the client prefers for responses)

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What is the "Safe" way to retry a request after a network failure?

choices.text: Retry with idempotent methods or documented safe-retry tokens (not blind POST repeats)
Always resend the same POST without checking server outcome

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What does "Separation of Concerns" achieve in web architecture?

choices.text: Splitting responsibilities so each part can evolve independently
Collapse routing, data access, and UI into one unmaintainable file
Remove HTTP APIs so only static files exist
Run all business rules exclusively inside the browser cache

Only on CDN edges with no JavaScript execution

choices.text: GET for safe reads of resource representations

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of a "Monolithic" architecture?

choices.text: Harder to scale or deploy pieces independently; failures/changes ripple system-wide

Prevents MongoDB from being used as the document store
Prevents the server from returning HTML responses

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What is "Idempotency"?

choices.text: Applying the same operation multiple times leaves the same end state
Only GET requests are permitted on public APIs
The property that payloads are always encrypted end-to-end
A requirement that all traffic uses WebSockets

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: Which layer handles "Business Logic" in the MERN stack?

choices.text: Express/Node routes and services (the server application tier)
Only the interactive MongoDB shell session
Only stylesheets and static asset folders

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: How does a server "listen" for requests?

choices.text: Bind to a TCP port and accept connections (e.g., `listen`)
Poll the filesystem for new files continuously
Perform DNS lookups for every static asset
Open Chrome DevTools on the developer machine

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What is a "Single-Page Application" (SPA)?

choices.text: One HTML shell; client-side routing swaps views without full page reloads
Every navigation performs a full server round-trip HTML reload
A deployment with zero JavaScript on the client
A mobile app distributed only as a native binary package

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What does the "Authorization" header typically contain?

choices.text: Credential material such as `Bearer <token>` or scheme-specific auth data
A copy of the JSON request body duplicated in headers
Legacy cookie names from obsolete HTTP specifications only
Client display metadata like resolution and color depth

questions.section: Section 1: S1: Web Applications and The Client-Server Model
questions.question: What is the "Single Source of Truth" for application data?

choices.text: The authoritative server-side store (typically the DB behind the API)
Whatever is newest in the browser tab cache
localStorage snapshots on each user device
Printed reference sheets maintained by the user

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

choices.text: Relative units like em or rem

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

choices.text: Making a site for one specific group

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

choices.text: Recognition rather than recall of objects and actions

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles
questions.question: What is the goal of the "Aesthetic and Minimalist Design" heuristic?

choices.text: Keep interfaces minimal—show what users need, hide noise

style (inline CSS string only, never objects)

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

choices.text: Big Design Up Front for every feature

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

questions.section: S2: UI/UX and Software Design Principles

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: Which type of conflict involves disagreements about work content and goals?

choices.text: Disagreements about interpersonal style, trust, or personalities
Tension rooted in how people relate, not the task definition itself
Disputes over formal logic proofs in specifications
Disagreements focused on the work goals, priorities, or deliverables

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: What is a characteristic of "Relationship Conflict"?

choices.text: Deep focus on defect triage and patch throughput metrics
Interpersonal friction, personality clashes, or strained working relationships
Sustained lift in measurable team innovation velocity
Alignment conversations strictly about milestone dates

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: Which conflict type is most likely to reduce team performance and satisfaction?

choices.text: Task-focused debate about priorities, scope, or technical approach
Relationship-driven friction that erodes trust and morale
Process disagreements about workflows, roles, or ceremonies

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: According to Tuckman’s stages, what is the first stage of team development?

choices.text: Storming (open conflict over roles and leadership)
Forming (initial orientation, polite uncertainty, goals unclear)
Norming (stable norms and cohesion after conflict)

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: In which stage of team development do members start to resolve differences and develop symbols of local identity?

choices.text: Politeness norms forming with little conflict yet
High-performing delivery with no further role negotiation
Norms solidify; cohesion improves after early friction
Orderly team wrap-up after project completion

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: What is the "Storming" stage characterized by?

choices.text: Open conflict as roles, influence, and approaches compete
Perfect harmony with no disagreements whatsoever
Immediate permanent disbanding of the team charter
Policy that only positive feedback may be spoken aloud

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: Which Thomas-Kilmann conflict mode is "High Assertiveness" and "Low Cooperativeness"?

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: What is the "Collaborating" mode in conflict management?

choices.text: High assertiveness and high cooperation toward an integrative solution
Low assertiveness paired with low cooperation (withdrawal)
Ignore the disagreement entirely without follow-up
Split the difference instantly without exploring interests

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: Which document outlines communication protocols and role assignments for a team?

choices.text: Team charter or working agreement (communication, roles, norms)
Gantt chart that only lists task durations
Individual résumé or CV for a single member
Accounts-payable invoice sent to the client

questions.section: S5: Misc. (Group Management)
questions.question: The "Compromising" mode is characterized by what?

choices.text: Maximum assertiveness with almost no cooperation
